name: react-router-expert
description: React Router 6+ expert for enterprise PWA routing, authentication guards, deep linking, and code-split navigation

can_do: Design hierarchical route structures for enterprise PWAs
Create deep-linkable, shareable URLs with query parameters
Set up route-based code splitting with lazy loading
Build nested route layouts with Outlet pattern
Extract and validate URL parameters and query strings
Implement breadcrumb navigation from route hierarchy
Handle 404 and error routes with error boundaries
Create type-safe route configurations with TypeScript
Optimize route performance with preloading and prefetching
Implement scroll restoration and navigation blocking
cannot_do: Implement server-side routing (use Next.js or Remix)
Handle authentication logic (use oauth2-expert or web-security-expert)
Design component architecture (use react architecture experts)

createBrowserRouter.statement: createBrowserRouter creates a router using the HTML5 History API with automatic data fetching, error handling, and code splitting support
createBrowserRouter.example: const router = createBrowserRouter([{ path: '/', element: <RootLayout />, children: [...] }]);

RouterProvider.statement: RouterProvider is the component that renders the router and enables routing in the application

Outlet.statement: Outlet renders child routes in a parent route's element, enabling nested route layouts

Navigate.statement: Navigate component performs declarative navigation and redirects when rendered

Link.statement: Link component renders an anchor tag that navigates without page reload

NavLink.statement: NavLink is a special Link that knows whether it's active, allowing active state styling

useNavigate.statement: useNavigate returns a navigate function for programmatic navigation with support for state, replace, and history manipulation
useNavigate.example: const navigate = useNavigate(); navigate('/cases/123', { state: { from: 'dashboard' } });

useParams.statement: useParams returns an object of URL parameters from the current route

useSearchParams.statement: useSearchParams returns current search params and a function to update them, similar to useState
useSearchParams.example: const [searchParams, setSearchParams] = useSearchParams(); const status = searchParams.get('status');

useLocation.statement: useLocation returns the current location object with pathname, search, hash, state, and key
useLocation.example: const location = useLocation(); const state = location.state as { from?: string };

useMatches.statement: useMatches returns an array of all matched routes, useful for building breadcrumbs or determining active routes
useMatches.example: const matches = useMatches(); const breadcrumbs = matches.filter(m => m.handle?.breadcrumb);

useBlocker.statement: useBlocker blocks navigation when condition is true, useful for warning about unsaved changes
useBlocker.example: const blocker = useBlocker(hasUnsavedChanges); if (blocker.state === 'blocked') { /* show confirmation */ }

route_objects.statement: Route objects are plain JavaScript objects defining route configuration, preferred over JSX routes for enterprise apps due to better TypeScript support and testability
route_objects.example: const routes: RouteObject[] = [{ path: '/', element: <Home />, children: [...] }];

nested_routes.statement: Nested routes use the children array to define route hierarchy, with parent routes rendering Outlet to display child routes

index_routes.statement: Index routes use index: true instead of path and render as the default child route when parent's path is matched exactly
index_routes.example: { path: 'cases', element: <Layout />, children: [{ index: true, element: <CaseList /> }] }

catch_all_routes.statement: Catch-all routes use path: '*' to match any unmatched routes, typically used for 404 pages

error_boundary_routes.statement: Routes can have errorElement property that renders when route errors occur, providing error boundary at route level

auth_guard_pattern.statement: Authentication guards are implemented as route elements that check authentication status and redirect to login or render Outlet for child routes
auth_guard_pattern.example: function AuthGuard() { const { isAuthenticated } = useAuth(); if (!isAuthenticated) return <Navigate to="/login" />; return <Outlet />; }

role_guard_pattern.statement: Role-based guards check user roles before rendering child routes, redirecting to unauthorized page if role requirements not met
role_guard_pattern.example: function RoleGuard({ requiredRole }) { const { hasRole } = useAuth(); if (!hasRole(requiredRole)) return <Navigate to="/unauthorized" />; return <Outlet />; }

permission_guard_pattern.statement: Permission guards provide fine-grained access control at the feature level using permission strings
permission_guard_pattern.example: function PermissionGuard({ requiredPermission }) { const { hasPermission } = useAuth(); if (!hasPermission(requiredPermission)) return <Navigate to="/unauthorized" />; return <Outlet />; }

guard_composition.statement: Guards can be composed by nesting route elements: outer guards for authentication, inner guards for authorization
guard_composition.example: { path: 'admin', element: <AuthGuard />, children: [{ element: <RoleGuard requiredRole="admin" />, children: [...] }] }

lazy_loading.statement: Routes can be code-split using React.lazy and dynamic imports, with Suspense providing fallback UI during loading
lazy_loading.example: const AdminPage = lazy(() => import('./pages/AdminPage')); { path: 'admin', element: <Suspense fallback={<Loading />}><AdminPage /></Suspense> }

route_preloading.statement: Route bundles can be preloaded on link hover or idle using dynamic imports before navigation

data_prefetching.statement: Data for routes can be prefetched using React Query prefetchQuery on hover before navigation

url_design_principles.statement: URLs should be RESTful, human-readable, and every application state should be URL-addressable for deep linking

query_param_usage.statement: Query parameters should be used for filters, sorting, pagination, and search - not path segments - to maintain shareable, bookmarkable URLs

state_in_url.statement: Critical application state (filters, current page, selected items) should be stored in URL query params to enable sharing and bookmarking
state_in_url.example: const [searchParams, setSearchParams] = useSearchParams(); setSearchParams({ status: 'open', page: '2' });

fetch_from_url.statement: Route components should always fetch data based on URL parameters, never assuming in-memory state exists, to support deep linking
fetch_from_url.example: const { id } = useParams(); const { data } = useQuery(['cases', id], () => fetchCase(id));

programmatic_navigation.statement: useNavigate returns a function that programmatically navigates, supporting path, state, replace option, and relative navigation

history_navigation.statement: navigate function can go back/forward in history using negative/positive numbers: navigate(-1) goes back, navigate(1) goes forward

navigation_state.statement: Navigation state is passed via state option and accessed via useLocation().state, not visible in URL but preserved during navigation
navigation_state.example: navigate('/cases', { state: { message: 'Case created!' } }); const state = useLocation().state;

replace_navigation.statement: Replace option replaces current history entry instead of pushing new one, preventing back button from returning to replaced entry

outlet_pattern.statement: Outlet component renders matched child route element, enabling layout components to wrap children with common UI

multiple_layouts.statement: Different route branches can have different layouts by nesting route elements with Outlet at each level
multiple_layouts.example: { path: 'app', element: <AppLayout />, children: [...] }, { path: 'admin', element: <AdminLayout />, children: [...] }

nested_outlets.statement: Layouts can nest multiple levels deep, each with their own Outlet, creating hierarchical UI structures
nested_outlets.example: RootLayout → AppLayout → CasesLayout → CaseDetail (3 levels of nesting)

url_parameters.statement: URL parameters are defined with : prefix in route path and accessed via useParams hook as object of string values
url_parameters.example: { path: 'cases/:id' }; const { id } = useParams<{ id: string }>();

query_parameters.statement: Query parameters are accessed via useSearchParams hook which returns URLSearchParams object and setter function
query_parameters.example: const [params, setParams] = useSearchParams(); const status = params.get('status');

type_safe_params.statement: TypeScript generics can type URL parameters, but values are always strings or undefined and must be validated
type_safe_params.example: const { id } = useParams<{ id: string }>(); if (!id) return <Navigate to="/cases" />;

error_boundaries.statement: Route errorElement catches errors in route rendering and provides fallback UI, integrating with useRouteError hook

useRouteError.statement: useRouteError hook returns error thrown in route, can be checked with isRouteErrorResponse for status code handling

404_handling.statement: 404 pages are implemented using path: '*' catch-all route at end of routes array

scroll_restoration.statement: ScrollRestoration component automatically restores scroll position on back/forward navigation

memoized_routes.statement: Route components can be wrapped with React.memo to prevent unnecessary re-renders when route doesn't change

memory_router.statement: MemoryRouter is used in tests to simulate routing without browser dependencies, accepting initialEntries array

testing_guards.statement: Route guards are tested by rendering routes with different auth states via provider wrappers
